title: Charlie's Wooden Scooter
summary: A ride-on toy scooter cut entirely from a single 1x10 with a printed pattern — no measuring, just trace and cut.
description:
This one lives or dies on the pattern transfer, not the woodworking. Every part — frame, seat, wheel wells, headlight, rockers — comes from a printed PDF template you tile together, trace onto the board, and cut out with a jigsaw. There's no cut list of dimensions because there doesn't need to be one; the pattern is the cut list.

Paint the pieces before assembly, not after — it's dramatically easier to get clean coverage into every curve and corner while the parts are still flat and separate. Assembly is just gluing the traced pieces together in order, with a few countersunk screws added discreetly if you want the scooter to come apart for storage or repair later. Rounded rockers on the bottom are what let a toddler rock the scooter back and forth, so keep those edges smooth and free of any sharp jigsaw chatter.

tools.note: Every part is a curved pattern piece — this is the whole build.
Optional, if you want the scooter to disassemble for storage or repair.
Every traced edge needs sanding smooth — this is a toy a toddler will hold and sit against.
For tracing the pattern — a sharp pencil indents the wood without ink bleeding through, so mistakes are easy to catch.

materials.name: Pine, 1x10, 8 ft
materials.note: Every part of the scooter comes from this one board.

materials.name: Non-toxic paint
materials.note: Choose a child-safe, non-toxic finish — this is a toy that gets chewed on.

steps.title: Print and transfer the pattern
steps.body: Print the PDF pattern pieces — they'll come out tiled across several pages, so tape them together carefully before tracing. Use a sharp pen or pencil to trace each piece onto the 1x10; a pencil is safer here since ink can bleed into the grain, while a pencil just leaves a clean indent line to follow.

steps.title: Cut and sand every piece
steps.body: Cut all the traced pieces out with a jigsaw. Sand every edge smooth now, while the parts are still flat and separate — it's much easier to get into every curve before assembly than after. Paint the pieces at this stage too, before you glue anything together; painting flat, separated pieces gives cleaner coverage than trying to brush into assembled corners later.

steps.title: Assemble the frame and front
steps.body: Fit the frame piece to the front piece and glue in place. Add a few discreetly placed countersunk screws if you'd like the scooter to be able to come apart later for storage or repair.

steps.title: Add the seat
steps.body: Glue and screw the seat piece onto the frame assembly.

steps.title: Add the wheel wells
steps.body: Attach the wheel well pieces to the sides of the frame.

steps.title: Add the headlight
steps.body: Glue the 7" length of 1/2" dowel into the front piece as the headlight.

steps.title: Add the base supports
steps.body: Attach the base support pieces underneath the frame for stability.

steps.title: Attach the rockers
steps.body: Glue and screw the rockers to the bottom last. These are what let the scooter rock — make sure both are identical and mounted level with each other, or the scooter will rock unevenly to one side.

images.alt: Painted wooden ride-on scooter toy with rounded rockers, cut from a single board pattern
